title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Saline Township pushes back on the massive Stargate data center amid unlimited tax breaks and local concerns.

lines.text: Good morning, and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's a Saturday morning here on July 18, and we're starting with big resistance in a small farming community.
Yeah, Saline Township residents are speaking out against that $16 billion Stargate project already under construction.
Exactly, and it ties right into the bigger fight over tax incentives, water, and what these projects mean for rural towns.
Let's unpack this lead story first.
Residents in the 2,400-person Saline Township say the Stargate data center will drain local water supplies, drive up utility costs, and erase the rural character they've known for generations.
How did we get here? What's the history behind this project?
It's tied to Oracle and OpenAI's massive plans in the Ann Arbor area. The project was pitched as a huge economic win, but locals are now seeing the downsides up close.
And today we learned Saline Township dropped its earlier $4.8 billion cap on incentives, handing the company a blank check on tax breaks.
Right, that reversal means decades of lost property-tax revenue with no upper limit. The money that would have gone to schools and roads is now off the table.
What does that actually mean for everyday taxpayers in Washtenaw County?
Higher electric bills and water rates are the main fears, plus the loss of farmland that has defined the area. These facilities use enormous amounts of both resources.
I keep hearing about gigawatts of power demand. How does that play out locally?
One large data center can pull as much power as a small city. Without new infrastructure paid for by the companies, ratepayers end up footing the bill for upgrades.
And the water angle feels especially urgent in a farming community.
Cooling these servers requires massive water withdrawals. Residents worry about wells running dry and impacts on agriculture that has been there for decades.
Who are the main players pushing this forward?
Oracle and its partners are driving the investment, while local officials had initially tried to set some limits before reversing course on the cap.
What happens next? Are there legal challenges or more public pushback coming?
Residents are already organizing, and this could set a precedent for other townships weighing similar deals. The unlimited tax break changes the financial stakes significantly.
Meanwhile, we're seeing moratoria popping up elsewhere as communities hit pause to study the impacts.
That's the statewide picture emerging today.
Let's move to quick hits. Hagar Township approved a one-year moratorium on new data centers to study noise, water, and power issues.
That gives nearby property owners a temporary shield while officials gather more information before any projects advance.
Warren in Macomb County is eyeing a six-month ban for the same reasons—grid strain and potential utility bill spikes.
Council discussion there signals growing local pushback against rapid buildouts without safeguards.
State Senator Andrews is reviving legislation to make operators pay for grid upgrades and disclose water use statewide.
The bill would also limit local tax breaks, offering ratepayers in every county some cost protections if it passes.
Yesterday Monroe County commissioners voted to pause all data-center proposals for now.
Residents near potential sites are worried about sudden spikes in power bills and water use, so the temporary ban buys time for study.
Also yesterday, Hyperscale Data faced direct questions on noise and power at a city council meeting.
Neighbors pressed for details on backup generators and cooling systems, getting their first public look at the project's local footprint.
Lake Township trustees approved a six-month moratorium on applications to write rules on noise, power, and water.
Neighbors there say the pause prevents rushed decisions that could affect property values and daily life.
New York's moratorium on large data centers could block or delay Genesee County's STAMP project.
Local officials are watching to see if the site loses its edge to other states, affecting hundreds of planned jobs.
That's a lot of local action this week.
It shows communities across Michigan are taking a harder look at the trade-offs.
On deck, any meetings listeners can attend this weekend?
No major public hearings listed for today or tomorrow, but we'll monitor township boards for updates next week.
Always good to stay engaged.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Saline Locals Resist $16B Stargate Project as Tax Breaks Expand Without Limit
